title: Functional Dimensional Regularization for O(N) Models
abstract: The novel functional dimensional regularization (FDR) scheme has proven capable of yielding results that are competitive with the state-of-the-art in the computation of critical exponents in $d=3$, while also reproducing those from the $\varepsilon$-expansion for the Ising and other universality classes. In this work, we show that this is not a mere coincidence: by applying the scheme to the $O(N)$ universality class, we explicitly derive the flow equations and obtain critical exponents that are comparable to those obtained with higher-order non-perturbative approaches. In this case, FDR retains the features already highlighted in previous works -- namely, its efficiency and rapid convergence.
summary: This paper investigates the Functional Dimensional Regularization (FDR) scheme applied to the O(N) universality class. The authors show that FDR is a lightweight and efficient method that can produce critical exponents comparable to much more computationally intensive non-perturbative renormalization group and conformal bootstrap methods. By explicitly deriving the flow equations, they prove that the scheme's success is not coincidental. The results demonstrate rapid convergence and high accuracy across a wide range of N values.
main_problem: The paper aims to extend the Functional Dimensional Regularization (FDR) scheme to the O(N) universality class and evaluate its efficiency and accuracy in calculating critical exponents.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that FDR provides highly efficient and accurate results for critical exponents in the O(N) model, with precision competitive with state-of-the-art methods like the Conformal Bootstrap and higher-order Non-Perturbative Renormalization Group.
method: The study utilizes Functional Dimensional Regularization (FDR) at the second-order derivative expansion (DE2) level, involving a polynomial expansion of the potential and wave-function renormalizations.
model_or_system: The study focuses on O(N) symmetric models/universality classes in three dimensions, including specific cases such as the Ising model (N=1), XY model (N=2), Heisenberg model (N=3), and self-avoiding random walks (N=0).
key_observables: Critical exponents: anomalous dimension (eta), correlation length exponent (nu), and correction-to-scaling exponent (omega).
important_parameters: Dimension d=3, number of field components N (ranging from -2 to 1000), and mass scale mu.
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed using a derivative expansion truncated at the second order (DE2) and a polynomial expansion of the potential and renormalization functions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows eta vs N; Figure 2 shows nu vs N; Figure 3 shows omega vs N, comparing FDR results against NPRG, Conformal Bootstrap, and large-N limits.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the FDR scheme, derives the flow equations for the O(N) model, presents numerical results for various N values, and compares these results against established benchmarks like NPRG and Conformal Bootstrap.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Estimate of the critical exponent η as a function of N in d = 3 at FDR-DE2 (blue) with nu = 11, NPRG-DE2 (red- square final result vs red-dot raw data), NPRG-DE4 (green- square final result vs green-dot raw data), large-N (magenta) and CB stars (black).

FIG. 3. Estimate of the critical exponent ω as a function of N in d = 3 at FDR-DE2 (blue) nu = 11, NPRG-DE2 (red-square final result vs red-dot raw data), NPRG-DE4 (green-square final result vs green-dot raw data), large-N (magenta) and CB stars (black).

FIG. 2. Estimate of the critical exponent ν as a function of N in d = 3 FDR-DE2 (blue) with nu = 11, NPRG-DE2 (red- square final result vs red-dot raw data), NPRG-DE4 (green- square final result vs green-dot raw data) and at large-N (ma- genta) and CB stars (black).
